text:
The seat is dying as a pricing unit, and most GTM teams haven't priced that in yet.

Think about what you're selling when you charge per seat. You're charging for a human to log in and do work. But the work is getting done by an agent that no one logs into. So what is the customer buying a seat for?

I've watched this play out in deal cycles over the last year. Buyers used to negotiate seat counts. Now they're asking what happens to their bill when their headcount drops 30% because an agent absorbed the workflow. If your answer is "you pay the same," you're going to lose the renewal.

The companies repricing around outcomes, tickets resolved, documents processed, revenue influenced, are pulling away on NRR. The ones holding the seat line are watching expansion flatten while their champions field uncomfortable questions from procurement.

The PLG vs sales-led debate from two years ago feels like arguing about store layouts while the mall empties out. The real question for 2026 is whether your pricing model survives the decoupling of value from logins.

If an agent did 80% of the work your product enables next year, would your contract still make sense to the buyer?

That's the conversation I'd be having with your pricing committee this quarter.

#SaaS #GTM #ProductStrategy #B2BSaaS #GrowthStrategy #ProductMarketing
